With the event neither televised nor live-streamed — and with the media barred from covering it in person — the HFPA unveiled this year’s winners to the public on its web site and via social media, a format that sorely lacked the snap and crackle of a live TV show featuring Hollywood’s funniest and most charismatic talent. “If laughter is the best medicine, @WestSideMovie is the cure for what ails you,” read the tweet announcing the climactic win for “West Side Story,” a tragic love story involving racial tensions that is not exactly intended to be hilarious. (The tweet was later revised to read “If music is the best medicine...”) A number of tweets failed to cite the work the winner was being honored for: “Save the drama for Will Smith, who takes home the #GoldenGlobe for Best Actor — Motion Picture — Drama,” read one about the “King Richard” star.  Awards  In the absence of a telecast, the Hollywood Foreign Press Assn. announced the winners for the 2022 Golden Globe Awards during a small “private” event.  For the HFPA, the muted event — the very existence of which many regular Globes viewers were likely unaware — represented a stunning stumble from the improbable perch of influence the small cadre of foreign entertainment journalists had enjoyed for decades. Fighting for its survival, the organization was determined to hand out its awards as it had for nearly 80 years.

Winners of the 79th Golden Globe Awards were announced Sunday evening via social media after the ceremony lost its broadcaster due to issues with its host, the Hollywood Foreign Press Association. The embattled HFPA came under fire after a Los Angeles Times investigation revealed in February 2021 ethical lapses and a stunning lack of diversity, including the lack of a single Black journalist in the 87-person group. Studios and PR firms threatened to boycott. Tom Cruise even returned his three Golden Globes, while other A-listers condemned the group on social media. The organization instead held its film awards Sunday night without a telecast, nominees, a red carpet, a host, press or even a livestream. Instead, members of the HFPA and some recipients of the group’s philanthropic grants gathered at the Beverly Hilton Hotel for a 90-minute private event, announcing the names of the film and television winners on the organization's social media feeds.

query: When does the 2022 Olympic Winter Games end?
positive:
In the brisk March of 2022, the world turned its gaze to Beijing, where the XXIV Olympic Winter Games unfolded from the 4th to the 20th. Commonly referred to as Beijing 2022, this grand international winter multi-sport event had been eagerly anticipated, with some competitions kicking off even earlier, on March 2nd. This marked the 24th edition of the Winter Olympic Games, a celebration of athletic prowess and global unity.

Beijing's journey to this moment began back in 2015 when it was chosen as the host city during the 128th IOC Session held in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. This was not merely a return to Olympic glory for the city; it was a historic occasion, as Beijing became the first city ever to host both the Summer and Winter Olympics. It was a significant milestone, following the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang County, South Korea, and the 2020 Summer Olympics in Tokyo, Japan, as the last of three consecutive Olympic events held in East Asia. The anticipation was palpable, as athletes from around the globe gathered, ready to etch their names into the annals of Olympic history.

On the evening of February 18, 2022, the grand closing ceremony of the 2022 Winter Olympics unfolded at the majestic Beijing National Stadium. This celebrated event was not just a farewell; it was a vibrant tapestry of cultural presentations, heartfelt closing remarks, and a momentous formal handover of the Olympic torch to Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo, the upcoming hosts of the 2026 Winter Olympics. 

The 2022 Winter Games were historic, featuring a remarkable 109 events across 15 disciplines within seven sports. Among the highlights were seven new medal events that captivated audiences, including the thrilling men's and women's big air freestyle competitions, the women's monobob, and mixed team contests in freestyle skiing aerials, ski jumping, and snowboard cross. Adding to the excitement was the mixed relay in short track speed skating. Each sport brought its own unique challenges, and numbers in parentheses reflected the intense competition, showcasing the number of medal events contested in each discipline.

The journey to these games began back in October 2016, when the International Ski Federation (FIS) revealed plans to allow women to compete in Nordic combined for the very first time, setting the stage for a historic moment in Beijing. Then, in November 2017, the FIS proposed three additional events for potential Olympic inclusion: a mixed team competition in ski jumping, along with men's and women's big air in freestyle skiing, hinting at an exciting evolution of the Winter Olympics to come.

As the final curtain fell on the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics, the air crackled with excitement amidst a backdrop of colorful fireworks illuminating the night sky. It was March 5, 2022, and the iconic Bird's Nest stadium, despite the biting cold, was alive with a small yet spirited crowd. They waved the Chinese flag and participated in a joyful wave, their enthusiasm palpable as they awaited the evening's grand festivities.

The closing ceremony marked the end of a tumultuous Olympic Games, one overshadowed by the specter of COVID-19 concerns, political tensions, and the specter of another Russian doping scandal. After 16 days of thrilling competition across 109 events, the Games concluded in a celebration that mirrored the subdued tone of the opening ceremony. 

In the stands, Chinese President Xi Jinping sat alongside International Olympic Committee President Thomas Bach, both men observing as the ceremonies unfolded before them. As members of Team China paraded proudly, flags waving in unison, the theme of snow and ice resonated throughout the performances, a fitting tribute to the winter sport spectacle that had captivated the world.

As the sun began to set on March 5, 2022, members of Team Denmark made their way into the grand Beijing National Stadium, ready to partake in the Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. The atmosphere was electric, filled with anticipation and pride as athletes gathered to celebrate their achievements over the past sixteen days.

In the midst of the celebration, the spotlight shone brightly on the medalists of the Men's Cross-Country Skiing 50km Mass Start Free event. Gold medallist Alexander Bolshunov of Team ROC stood proudly at the center, flanked by silver medallist Ivan Yakimushkin to his left and bronze medallist Simen Hegstad Krueger of Team Norway to his right. Each athlete held their medals aloft, symbols of their hard-fought victories.

As the ceremony unfolded, performers danced gracefully around the iconic Olympic Cauldron, which flickered with flames, casting a warm glow over the stadium. The scene was one of unity and celebration, a testament to the spirit of the games. A general view of the stadium revealed the cauldron's majesty, surrounded by the jubilant atmosphere that enveloped all in attendance, marking a memorable conclusion to the Winter Olympics.

As the snow blanketed the landscape, the season unfolded for five long months, beginning in November. During this time, Chongli became a bustling hub of activity, hosting an impressive thirty-six competitions and events, including the prestigious Far East Cup and the delightful Children Skiing International Festival. The atmosphere buzzed with excitement as twenty-three skiing camps emerged, drawing in around 3,800 eager youths ready to embrace the winter sports spirit.

The groundwork for all the venues had been laid back in November 2016, and by the end of 2020, they stood completed, ready to welcome test events. On a crisp day, 26 October 2021, the design for the Games' medals was revealed, inspired by the iconic medals of the 2008 Summer Olympics while intertwining elements of Chinese astronomy and astrology, perfectly aligning with the joyous Chinese New Year festivities.

As January 2022 rolled around, anticipation grew with the unveiling of the uniforms for the medal presenters at the ceremonies. This creative endeavor was a collaboration between the Central Academy of Fine Arts and the Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, resulting in a stunning display of artistry.

The journey of the Olympic torch began in the ancient land of Greece on 18 October 2021. Soon after, on 20 October, it was announced that the local leg would ignite on 10 February and conclude with the grand Opening Ceremonies on 12 February 2022, with the relay making stops only in two cities: the vibrant Beijing and the picturesque Zhangjiakou. However, amidst the celebrations, a wave of activism stirred as protestors made their voices heard at the Olympic torch lighting ceremony in Greece, casting a shadow over the festivities.

In the year 2019, a remarkable transformation unfolded with the completion of the Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway. This engineering marvel significantly reduced travel times, allowing passengers to journey from Beijing to Yanqing in just 20 minutes and to Zhangjiakou in a mere 50 minutes. As the world turned its gaze toward the Olympic Winter Games in Beijing in March 2022, excitement filled the air, especially with the introduction of seven thrilling new events. Women’s monobob, freestyle skiing big air for both men and women, alongside mixed team events in short track speed skating, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials, and snowboard cross, were set to make history. This edition of the Games promised to be the most gender-balanced ever, showcasing a record 45.44 percent female athletes and a plethora of women’s events, surpassing all previous Winter Games.

As the festivities coincided with the Chinese New Year, also known as the Spring Festival, the atmosphere was charged with joy and celebration. Local residents embraced the opportunity to welcome athletes from around the world, inviting them to partake in the vibrant festivities, experience the stunning landscapes, and immerse themselves in the rich cultures of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou. Amidst the cheers and excitement, a sense of unity and sportsmanship prevailed, marking a memorable chapter in Olympic history. A comprehensive list of teams and the medals they earned would capture the achievements of this unforgettable event.

As the sun set over Beijing National Stadium on March 15, 2022, the air buzzed with excitement during the Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. Performers gracefully danced, their movements weaving a tapestry of celebration and achievement as Day 16 of the games came to a close. Among the festivities, children from Milan and Cortina captivated the audience, holding a globe aloft as a symbol of the handover ceremony, marking the next chapter in Olympic history. The Olympic Cauldron flickered in the background, its flames a reminder of the spirit of competition that had filled the stadium. In the midst of the vibrant spectacle, a correction surfaced regarding the medal standings; a previous report had mistakenly placed the U.S. in third, when in reality, they stood fifth in total medal count. Brian Mann's contribution to this narrative served to clarify the facts as the curtain fell on an unforgettable event.

On a momentous Sunday, the leader of the International Olympic Committee, Bach, performed a symbolic act by transferring the Olympic flag to the mayors of Milan and Cortino, who would proudly host the 2026 Winter Games. As the atmosphere buzzed with anticipation, the figure of Xi Jinping, President of China, illuminated the scene on a grand screen, just moments before the Olympic Flag soared in the air during the closing ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics. This spectacle unfolded at the iconic Beijing National Stadium, known as the Bird's Nest, on March 20, 2022. 

The closing ceremony was a vibrant tapestry of performances, highlighted by children carrying enchanting snowflake lanterns, their joyful faces reflecting the spirit of the games. In this same arena, the children captivated the audience with their spirited display, marking the end of the Olympic journey. The flag bearers, a symbol of unity and national pride, gracefully made their entrance into the stadium, drawing cheers from the crowd as they participated in this historic moment of celebration.

In the heart of Oslo, a storm brewed between the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and the Norwegian government. The IOC had made a bold demand: control over all advertising space in the city, a request that left many scratching their heads. After all, Norway's government did not own or control every inch of advertising space in Oslo, nor did it have the authority to grant a foreign private organization exclusive rights to the city's private properties. In a tale as old as time, the two Spanish giants prepared to clash once more in the Spanish Super Cup final, marking their eighth encounter in this storied competition. Real Madrid, having tasted victory six times and endured just one defeat, stood poised for battle. On a sunlit Sunday, the King Fahd International Stadium in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, would set the stage for the defending champions, Barcelona, as they faced their arch-rivals.

The Spanish Super Cup, once a simple duel between two teams, had transformed into a grand four-team tournament since the 2019-20 season, drawing together the champions and runners-up of La Liga and the Copa del Rey. Barcelona, the most successful club in the history of this tournament with an impressive collection of 13 titles, carried the weight of their past glories into this match. Yet, a shadow loomed; the Blaugrana had suffered defeat in six of their seven finals against Real Madrid and had yet to secure a title in this new format.

The road to this highly anticipated El Clasico of 2023 had been paved with triumphs in the semi-finals. Barcelona had faced Valencia, emerging victorious in a gripping penalty shootout after Karim Benzema’s opening goal was equalized in the second half. Meanwhile, Real Madrid had a rollercoaster match against Real Betis, taking the lead twice through the strikes of Robert Lewandowski and Ansu Fati, yet ultimately needing to rely on penalties to secure their place in the final. As history awaited to be written, both teams readied themselves for a showdown that would echo through the ages.

In the heart of Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, the King Fahd International Stadium buzzed with anticipation on January 16, 2022, as two storied football clubs prepared to clash in the 2022 Supercopa de España final. This match would determine the champion of the 2021–22 Supercopa de España, marking the 38th iteration of this prestigious annual competition. The atmosphere was electric, a palpable tension in the air, as Athletic Bilbao, the runners-up of the 2020–21 Copa del Rey, faced off against Barcelona, the second-place finishers in the 2020–21 La Liga. It was a historic meeting, the first time these fierce rivals had vied for a trophy since the 1958 Copa del Generalísimo Final.

As the whistle blew, history was rewritten. Barcelona emerged victorious, sealing a 2–0 win and claiming their twelfth Supercopa de España title. Luka Modrić, a standout performer on the field, was named Man of the Match, a testament to his pivotal role in the game. The match officials kept a watchful eye, with Raúl Cabañero Martínez and José Gallego García serving as assistant referees, while Isidro Díaz de Mera Escuderos took on the duties of the fourth official. Pau Cebrián Devis stood ready as the reserve assistant referee, and in the background, David Medié Jiménez and Javier Alberola Rojas provided their expertise as part of the video assistant referee team. The rules of the match were set, but the drama that unfolded on the pitch would be remembered long after the final whistle blew.

The stage was set for the 2023 Spanish Super Cup, a thrilling tournament that awaited its contenders. The recent draw between Atlético Madrid and Sevilla on a fateful Sunday had sealed Barcelona’s fate, ensuring they would finish second in LaLiga and claim the last coveted spot in the Super Cup. As January approached, excitement buzzed around the arid sands of Saudi Arabia, where the tournament was to unfold. Joining Barcelona in the Middle East were the formidable LaLiga champions, Atlético Madrid, the Copa del Rey victors, Real Betis, and the Copa runners-up, Valencia.

With the rules of the Super Cup as their guide, the semi-finals promised intense matchups: the holders of the Copa del Rey, Real Betis, were set to clash with Barcelona, while Atlético Madrid would face off against Valencia, the losing finalists from the Copa. The potential for a Clásico in the final loomed large, a highly anticipated encounter that had yet to grace the expanded format of the four-team tournament. In this new chapter of the Super Cup, Barça and Atlético had met only once, during the semi-finals of the current season, where Los Colchoneros triumphed over the Blaugrana with a narrow 3-2 victory before going on to defeat Athletic Club in the final.

query: What is the price for a 30-second spot during the Super Bowl 2022?
positive:
As the sun began to set on that fateful Sunday, anticipation filled the air, for a star-studded lineup of Super Bowl LVII commercials was poised to grace the screens of millions. Fans, eager for the thrilling clash between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Philadelphia Eagles, knew that beyond the fierce competition lay a treasure trove of iconic advertisements that had become a beloved part of the spectacle. This year, the stakes were higher than ever, with the price for a mere 30-second commercial soaring to a staggering record of $7 million, according to Forbes. Advertisers poured their creativity and resources into these fleeting moments, hoping to leave an indelible mark on the viewers' minds.

Reflecting on the evolution of this phenomenon, one could trace the rise in costs through the years: in 2022, a 30-second spot had commanded $5.5 million, a notable leap from the $4.5 million of 2021. The figures painted a clear picture of the growing allure of this grand event. Yet, amidst the excitement of the game and the barrage of commercials, there lingered a peculiar rule that shrouded the event in a veil of secrecy. Known to many as the "Big Game," the Super Bowl was so protected by the NFL’s trademark since 1969 that advertisers and media personnel were required to tread carefully, paying a price to invoke its true name. Thus, the day unfolded, a tapestry woven with thrilling plays and unforgettable ads, each vying for a place in the hearts of viewers across the nation.

In the landscape of commercial advertising, the journey through the decades tells a remarkable story. Back in 1967, during the very first Super Bowl, advertisers could secure a 30-second spot for a modest sum of $37,500 on NBC and $42,500 on CBS. Fast forward to 1970, and the price had climbed to $78,200. By 1975, it reached $107,000, and in 1980, the cost had surged to $222,000. The years rolled on, with 1985 seeing a significant jump to $525,000 and then to an impressive $700,400 in 1990. As we look to the present, according to AdAge, companies are now faced with a staggering price tag for a mere 30 seconds of airtime during the Super Bowl—an unprecedented $7 million, a steep rise from the $5.8 million that advertisers paid for spots during Super Bowl LVI just a year prior.

In the world of sports, fans often find themselves tuning out commercials, but there exists a singular occasion when viewers eagerly anticipate the advertisements: the Super Bowl. Super Bowl 56 is set to unfold with an electrifying matchup between the Bengals and the Rams, and as the halftime show approaches, excitement builds for a star-studded rap performance featuring legends like Dr. Dre, Snoop Dogg, Eminem, Mary J. Blige, and Kendrick Lamar. Amidst the thrill of the game, a plethora of new commercials will vie for attention, each vying for a place in the hearts of viewers, some destined to become iconic. However, securing a commercial spot during this prestigious event comes at a steep price.

As the year unfolded, the automotive industry found itself in the midst of a significant shift. Eric Haggstrom, the director of business intelligence for Advertiser Perceptions, shed light on this phenomenon, suggesting that the root of the decline in automotive ads was not merely tied to the grand spectacle of the Super Bowl, but rather to the myriad challenges plaguing the automotive sector. 

"The auto industry has been battered by supply chain issues, inflation eating into consumer budgets, and rising interest rates that have made car payments dramatically more expensive," he explained to CNBC. Haggstrom pointed out that several automakers had curbed their advertising expenditures in recent years, a decision driven by dwindling product availability stemming from tight inventories during the coronavirus pandemic. 

Moreover, newer players in the automotive market often chose to follow in Tesla's footsteps, opting not to spend on advertising at all. Despite these challenges, eight auto brands made their presence known during last year's Super Bowl, with familiar names like GM and Kia making a return. SAN FRANCISCO (KGO) -- Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry was honored with the Sports Illustrated Sportsperson of the Year award Thursday night in San Francisco. Curry won it with his teammates after the 2017-2018 season but is the solo recipient in 2022. "I grew up reading it, collecting it, and to know that in 2022 I'm on there it's pretty crazy to think about," Curry said. Pretty crazy, even for a four-time NBA Champion and two-time NBA most valuable player who has changed the game with his long range shots, but someone who says the success comes with hard work. MORE: Steph Curry, Kevin Durant lead NBA quotes of the week "I hope people understand the work that goes into it the constant sacrifice and all, that you can't just fast forward to the finished product, but it is special to know that you kind of changed the game," said Curry. And leave it to his teammate Draymond Green to say something that many here were thinking: it's about time they give him the award. "It's well deserved, possibly long overdue, but I'm happy to be here and watch him be honored today," Green said.

Ken Griffey Jr. will be the recipient of the 2023 Royal Brougham Sports Legend Award, the Seattle Sports Commission announced Friday morning. The Mariners Hall of Famer is scheduled to accept the award at the 88th annual Sports Star of the Year Awards Show on Feb. 28 at The Westin Seattle. “I’m honored to accept the Royal Brougham Sports Legend Award and join this roster of incredibly influential figures in Washington sports,” Griffey Jr. said in a statement. “Seattle is a very special place to my family and me, and I look forward to celebrating with and recognizing the accomplishments of fellow award winners, nominees, and the sports community.” Griffey began his major-league career with the Mariners as a 19-year-old in 1989 and grew to become one of the most popular athletes in American sports in the 1990s. He was elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame in 2016 after a 22-year career in which he hit 630 home runs, won 10 Gold Gloves and was named to 13 All-Star Games. He is now a member of the Mariners and Sounders FC ownership groups, and is a senior adviser to MLB commissioner Rob Manfred.

query: Who are the recipients of the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage?
positive: On January 21, 2022, a significant moment unfolded as Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Michael Johnson—the first Black students at Georgia Tech—alongside Ronald Yancey, the institution’s first Black graduate, were honored with the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. This prestigious award, established in 2010 in memory of Tech alumnus and former Atlanta Mayor Ivan Allen Jr., is sustained through a generous grant from the Wilbur and Hilda Glenn Family Foundation, offering a $100,000 stipend to its recipients. The prize's inaugural ceremony took place in March 2011, celebrating exceptional leaders who, much like Mayor Allen, embrace personal risks to uplift the lives of others. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera remarked on the determination of Greene, Long, Johnson, and Yancey, who faced numerous challenges and adversities, proving to the world that Black students indeed belonged at Georgia Tech. Their brave journey carved a path for the thousands of Black Yellow Jackets who have since graduated. Greene, Long, and Johnson, immortalized as the “three pioneers” in the Harrison Square sculpture representing their first day at the institute, embarked on their academic journey in the fall of 1961. While their arrival did not incite the violence seen at other southern universities, the road ahead was far from straightforward.

In the bustling halls of the Institute, the echoes of honored names resonate, such as House of Representatives member John Lewis and former U.S. President and Tech alumnus Jimmy Carter. It may seem unusual for the nomination committee and the President of Tech to bestow an award upon a student of the Institute, yet this decision comes at a pivotal moment, celebrating 60 years of Black student enrollment at Tech. The Institute has come to recognize that its true strength lies in its diversity. Figures like Thomas Jefferson, Michael Smith, the late George Harris, and Ronald Yancey carved out their legacies during their time at Tech, and the social courage they embodied continues to shape the campus atmosphere today. Their remarkable contributions have laid the groundwork for countless generations of Black Yellow Jackets who followed in their footsteps. A special award symposium to honor these four pioneers is set to take place on April 20, 2022, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. in the elegant Biltmore Ballroom within Tech Square.

In a moment that would resonate through the halls of Georgia Tech for generations to come, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams stepped onto campus as the very first Black students in 1961. Alongside them was Samuel Turner, who would later become the institution's first Black graduate. Their courageous journey, marked by determination and resilience, has now earned them the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage, a prestigious honor that places them among the ranks of noteworthy figures like Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter, John Lewis, and Andrew Young.

As their likenesses stand proudly enshrined in the sculptures of Harrison Square, these men are reminded not only of their groundbreaking achievements but also of the struggles they faced. “The Ivan Allen Jr. Prize recognizes exemplary, courageous leaders — those who, like Mayor Allen, take on personal risks in an effort to improve the lives of others,” noted Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera, echoing the spirit of their legacy. Greene, Long, Williams, and Turner confronted numerous hazards and adversities to assert their rightful place in academia, breaking down barriers for countless Black Yellow Jackets who would follow in their footsteps.

Their arrival at Georgia Tech, though not marked by the violence seen at other southern universities, was fraught with challenges. Dubbed the “three pioneers” in the sculpture that captures their first day, Greene, Long, and Williams forged a path that would change the landscape of education forever, laying the groundwork for future generations to thrive in a once-unimaginable environment.

In the year 1961, a significant chapter unfolded at Georgia Tech as three Black students stepped onto campus, marking a momentous occasion in history. They became the first to integrate peacefully in the Deep South, an achievement that took place without the intervention of a court order. The surrounding atmosphere was charged with civil unrest, with college campuses across the South grappling with tensions. Fast forward to April 20, 2022, at the elegant Biltmore Ballroom in Atlanta, where the legacy of these trailblazers was commemorated. Michael Johnson, James Smith, and the late David Brown, alongside Samuel Thompson, the Institute's first Black graduate, were honored with the prestigious Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage.

The celebration included a thought-provoking symposium led by Charlayne Hunter-Gault, a previous recipient of the same prize in 2020. On stage, Smith, Johnson, and Thompson shared their experiences of integrating the Institute, recounting their personal journeys and the ongoing fight for racial equity and inclusion in higher education across the nation. Despite the outward appearance of peaceful integration, their stories unveiled the harsh realities of racism they faced. They spoke of the isolation that accompanied their groundbreaking role, feeling disconnected from the majority of their peers during those early days. Yet, amid the challenges, a spark of hope illuminated their words. Johnson expressed gratitude for the students who followed in their footsteps, ensuring that future generations of Black students would find a sense of belonging at Tech. “I’m so thankful for those who continue to matriculate here and carry on the tradition,” he reflected warmly. As the event unfolded, a video message from Atlanta Mayor Andre Dickens, a proud Georgia Tech chemical engineering graduate, added an extra layer of significance to the celebration, bridging past and present in a shared commitment to progress.

On a cool February day in 2022, Tehreem Hussain shared a story that echoed through the halls of history. Beneath the looming presence of the Tech Tower in Harrison Square stood the sculpture of the “Three Pioneers,” a tribute to a pivotal moment in the Institute's journey with integration and race relations. It was September 27, 1961, when three brave black students took their seats in the classrooms of the Institute, marking a groundbreaking moment as Tech became the first higher education institution to integrate in the Deep South. This act of peaceful integration, achieved without a court order, was a bold step into uncharted territory, for at that time, black students often faced violence and turmoil on campuses across the South.

In a fitting recognition of their courage and trailblazing spirit, Tech’s first black students—Samuel Carter, James Wright, and the late Michael Johnson—along with the first black graduate, Thomas Bennett, were honored as recipients of the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. Established in 2010 to honor the legacy of the beloved Tech alumnus and former Atlanta mayor, Ivan Allen Jr., this award symbolizes the enduring commitment to the principle of bettering the human condition through progress and service. Much like Mayor Allen himself, those who receive this prestigious award have dedicated their lives to upholding moral principles, often at great personal risk to their careers and safety.

The Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage is an international award established in 2010 by the Georgia Institute of Technology[1] in recognition of the late Ivan Allen Jr.  A Georgia Tech alumnus, Allen became a pivotal leader during America's struggle for racial integration during the 1960s. While mayor of Atlanta, Georgia, USA (1962–1970), Allen risked his place in society, his political future, and his life when he testified before the U.S. Senate Commerce Committee in support of what became the Civil Rights Act of 1964.[2]  The Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage recognizes individuals who, like Mayor Allen, stand up for moral principles and render service to humanity without regard for personal or professional peril. Recipients positively affect public discourse in spite of the risk to their careers, their livelihoods, and even their lives.[3]  The Prize is funded in perpetuity by a grant from the Wilbur and Hilda Glenn Family Foundation. In the chilly month of January 2022, whispers began to circulate as The Verge unveiled a tantalizing secret: Google was hard at work on an ambitious AR headset. This innovative device, codenamed Project Nova, was designed to merge the digital realm with the tangible world through its outward-facing cameras that blended computer graphics with a live video feed. Nestled away in a highly secure facility in the San Francisco Bay Area, the project was under the watchful eye of Bavor. The headset, powered by the Android operating system and a custom chip reminiscent of Tensor, was anticipated to make its grand debut in 2024, coinciding with the launch of Project Starline. Among the key figures involved in this venture were Shahram Izadi, Eddie Chung, Scott Huffman, Kurt Akeley, and Lucovsky, each contributing their expertise to this cutting-edge technology. An early prototype of the headset bore a striking resemblance to the North Focals, hinting at its potential.

As the months rolled on, a new chapter unfolded in March when The Information reported that Google was set to acquire Raxium, an AR hardware startup, for a staggering $1 billion. This acquisition was just the beginning, as Google planned to pursue further purchases to bolster their work in augmented and mixed reality. By April, the acquisition was finalized, marking a significant step forward for the tech giant.

Then, in May 2022, during the highly anticipated Google I/O keynote, the company unveiled a fresh iteration of Nova—this time resembling a stylish pair of eyeglasses equipped with live translation capabilities. As summer approached, Google commenced public testing of these intriguing prototypes across the United States in August, with plans to expand their reach into Canada by October. Yet, amidst this whirlwind of innovation, a sense of frustration brewed among the employees, as the ever-shifting strategies of Google executives regarding Project Nova left many feeling unsettled.

In a world where technology constantly evolves, Google is poised to unveil its latest innovation: the Project Nimbus headset. This device promises to be a formidable contender in the realm of Augmented Reality (AR), stepping into the spotlight alongside the Metaverse and the cutting-edge advancements from Microsoft and Apple. Once focused on bringing the DayDream VR headsets and Google Glasses to market, the tech giant has shifted its gaze, perhaps inspired by the burgeoning AR landscape, to join the ranks of industry leaders like Facebook, Microsoft, and Apple.

Project Nimbus, as it is known, has an intriguing backstory. The name itself carries an air of mystery, hinting at the excitement surrounding its development. With the potential to incorporate advanced outer camera technology, this headset aims to seamlessly blend computer graphics with real-world visuals, creating an immersive experience for users. Reports suggest that the headset will operate wirelessly, liberating users from the constraints of tethered connections.

As the narrative unfolds, it becomes clear that Project Nimbus is not just about the hardware; it's a marriage of innovation and design. Resembling a pair of ski goggles adorned with cameras, this headset is engineered to function without external power. At its core lies the GS101 chipset, the same Silicon technology that powered last year’s Pixel 6 smartphone. However, the visual experience promises to be even more breathtaking, with graphic-intensive visuals sourced from a separate point. This setup necessitates an active internet connection, allowing data centers to remotely render stunning graphics for users.

As the project progresses, Google has revealed glimpses of the talented team working tirelessly behind the scenes. With the design reminiscent of ski goggles, the excitement surrounding Project Nimbus continues to build, hinting at a new era of augmented experiences on the horizon.

In the bustling halls of Google, a whisper began to circulate about an ambitious venture known as Project Zenith. This was no ordinary project; it was the codename for an unreleased augmented reality headset that the tech giant had been meticulously crafting. Designed to mimic the appearance of everyday eyeglasses, the headset was slated for a grand unveiling in 2024. Yet, as fate would have it, the dream was abruptly dashed in early 2023.

The shadow of Google Glass loomed large over the company ever since its highly publicized failure in 2013. The backlash had been swift, with critics decrying the smartglasses for their privacy and ethical dilemmas. In the aftermath, Google executives were wary, hesitant to dip their toes back into the waters of wearable AR technology. However, the tides began to shift by 2021, when the spark of innovation reignited. The excitement reached a peak during the 2022 Google I/O keynote when the company finally unveiled a version of Zenith to the world. Yet, just as hope seemed to flourish, the winds of change swept through the company. In the tumultuous landscape of 2023, amidst widespread layoffs and internal chaos, news broke that Google had decided to abandon Project Zenith. What had once seemed like a promising return to the realm of smartglasses now lay dormant, a reminder of the precarious nature of innovation. The journey had begun with the ambitious introduction of Google Glass a decade prior, and though it had met with harsh criticism, it had also set the stage for the ongoing pursuit of wearable technology.

In the realm of technology, Google had long cast its gaze upon the promising world of augmented reality (AR). The journey began with Google Glass, a venture that ultimately stumbled, earning the notorious nickname “Glassholes” due to its less-than-stellar execution. However, hope flickered anew with whispers of a second endeavor — Project Nova. This new initiative, as revealed by a report from The Verge, marks Google's latest attempt to carve its niche in AR. 

The seeds of Project Nova were sown back in 2020 when Google acquired North, a company renowned for its expertise in smart glasses. This acquisition hinted at the possibility of a revamped version of smart headwear. According to two insiders familiar with the project, the anticipated launch for these innovative glasses is set for 2024. In contrast to Google Glass, which offered only a modest display over one eye, the proposed design of Project Nova is destined to harness a wealth of data, significantly enhancing the functionality of the AR headset. Yet, the report maintains an air of mystery, for Project Nova remains cloaked in secrecy.

In a quiet corner of the tech world, Google made the decision to pull the plug on a project that had consumed years of effort: a pair of augmented-reality glasses, codenamed Nova. As the researchers at Meta began to harness the potential of the AQP, they embarked on training expansive models in natural-language processing (NLP) and computer vision. Their ultimate goal was to train models featuring trillions of parameters that could serve various functions across Meta’s platforms—from sophisticated content-moderation algorithms designed to identify hate speech on Facebook and Instagram to the augmented-reality features that would one day populate the metaverse.

The AQP proved to be a versatile ally, capable of training models that could interpret multimodal signals, discerning whether an action, sound, or image posed a threat or was harmless. Through these advancements, Meta asserted that they could not only enhance user safety across their current services but also pave the way for a secure environment in the expansive realms of the metaverse.

RSC's storage capabilities are equally remarkable, boasting a staggering 175 petabytes of Pure Storage FlashArray, supplemented by 46 petabytes of cache storage housed in Penguin Computing Altus systems, and an additional 10 petabytes from Pure Storage FlashBlade. In the heart of the tech world, a remarkable machine was coming to life, one that would revolutionize the way computer vision workflows were processed—20 times faster than ever before. To accommodate the colossal training datasets and enhance performance, the installation was set to expand. In 2013, a significant chapter in the evolution of artificial intelligence began with the establishment of the Facebook AI Research lab, known as FAIR. This innovative hub set forth on a journey that would yield remarkable advancements, crafting tools for chatbot design, pioneering methods for enabling AI systems to forget unnecessary information, and even developing ‘synthetic skin’ that endowed robots with the sense of touch. Among its many achievements, the lab’s crowning glory emerged in the form of PyTorch, an open-source deep learning framework that quickly became a cornerstone in the field, embraced by developers and data scientists across a multitude of platforms. 

As the years unfolded, Meta took a bold step in 2017, unveiling its first dedicated AI supercomputer, constructed with an impressive 22,000 Nvidia V100 GPUs. However, this machine soon found itself overshadowed by its successor, the RSC, which Meta claimed was capable of delivering three times the performance in large-scale natural language processing workflows while utilizing less than half of its eventual hardware footprint. The initial phase of this ambitious project featured 760 Nvidia DGX A100 server systems, boasting a total of 6,080 GPUs, all interconnected through Nvidia’s Quantum 200 Gb/s InfiniBand fabric. 

The intricate storage architecture was no less impressive, equipped with 185PB of all-flash memory from Pure Storage and an additional 46PB of cache storage distributed across Penguin Computing Altus servers. Training data flowed seamlessly through FAIR’s specially designed storage service, the AI Research Hub, also known as AIRHub. As the RSC project neared completion, anticipation grew, for the same InfiniBand fabric would soon link an astounding 16,000 GPUs, marking this as the largest DGX A100 deployment ever witnessed.

In the heart of innovation, a powerful new supercomputer was taking shape, destined to be served by a remarkable caching and storage system boasting a staggering 16 TB/s of bandwidth. This technological marvel was anticipated to unleash nearly 5 exaflops of mixed precision compute power. Kevin Lee, the technical program manager at Facebook, alongside software engineer Shubho Sengupta, shared their vision in a recent blog post. “We wanted this infrastructure to be capable of training models with over a trillion parameters on data sets as vast as an exabyte,” they explained, offering a perspective on the colossal scale of their ambition. “To put it into context, that’s equivalent to 36,000 years of high-quality video.”

Unlike its predecessor, which relied solely on open-source and publicly available data sets, Meta’s latest machine would harness real-world training data gathered directly from the users of the company’s platforms. This shift in approach led to the design of the Quantum Supercomputer (QSC) with privacy and security firmly at its core. Isolated from the vast expanse of the Internet, the supercomputer boasted no direct inbound or outbound connections; data traffic was carefully contained within Meta’s production data centers. Every piece of user data was anonymized, and the entire pathway from storage systems to the GPUs was cloaked in encryption.

With hopeful eyes on the horizon, Lee and Sengupta envisioned a future shaped by the QSC. “We hope QSC will help us build entirely new AI systems that can, for instance, enable real-time voice translations for large groups, each person speaking a different language. This way, they can seamlessly collaborate on a research project or engage in an AR game together.” The promise of collaboration and connectivity through this technological powerhouse was only just beginning to unfold.

In the vast world of supercomputers, the variations in their construction are as diverse as the challenges they tackle. These colossal machines often incorporate both central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs), yet the manufacturers of these chips and the intricate wiring connecting them differ significantly. To gauge the prowess of supercomputers, the industry relies on a benchmark known as floating-point operations per second, more commonly referred to as flops. This measure reveals how many simple equations a supercomputer can solve in just one second.

As the latest Top500 list shows, the crown for the world’s fastest all-around supercomputer goes to Fugaku, a remarkable creation from Japan. Interestingly, Fugaku does not utilize any GPUs, but it boasts an astonishing peak speed of 442 petaflops, translating to 442 thousand trillion operations every second. This speed is truly impressive. However, machines like Fugaku are also evolving to cater to the needs of artificial intelligence, prompting Top500 to introduce a new benchmark tailored for AI applications. Since machine learning algorithms don’t demand the same level of precision as scientific computations, this new AI benchmark employs a less stringent measure. Under this standard, Fugaku achieves peak speeds that soar above an exaflop, which equates to a staggering million trillion operations per second. This distinction is what defines an AI supercomputer.

Turning our attention back to Google, it’s worth noting that most of the machines listed on the Top500 are operated by governments and universities. In contrast, private supercomputers, such as Turing and the one developed by OpenAI and Microsoft, remain absent from this prestigious list. Thus, when it comes to performance, we must rely on the assurances of these companies.

Meta pulled the plug on a large-scale rollout of the custom chip, which was planned for 2022, and instead placed orders for billions of dollars’ worth of Nvidia GPUs that required major redesigns of several of its data centers. In an effort to turn things around, Meta made plans to start developing a more ambitious in-house chip, due out in 2025, capable of both training AI models and running them. And that was the main topic of today’s presentation. Meta calls the new chip the Meta Training and Inference Accelerator, or MTIA for short, and describes it as a part of a “family” of chips for accelerating AI training and inferencing workloads. (“Inferencing” refers to running a trained model.) The MTIA is an ASIC, a kind of chip that combines different circuits on one board, allowing it to be programmed to carry out one or many tasks in parallel. An AI chip Meta custom-designed for AI workloads. Image Credits: Meta An AI chip Meta custom-designed for AI workloads. Image Credits: Meta “To gain better levels of efficiency and performance across our important workloads, we needed a tailored solution that’s co-designed with the model, software stack and the system hardware,” Bjorlin continued. “This provides a better experience for our users across a variety of services.” Custom AI chips are increasingly the name of the game among the Big Tech players.

At a virtual event this morning, Meta lifted the curtains on its efforts to develop in-house infrastructure for AI workloads, including generative AI like the type that underpins its recently launched ad design and creation tools. It was an attempt at a projection of strength from Meta, which historically has been slow to adopt AI-friendly hardware systems — hobbling its ability to keep pace with rivals such as Google and Microsoft. “Building our own [hardware] capabilities gives us control at every layer of the stack, from datacenter design to training frameworks,” Alexis Bjorlin, VP of Infrastructure at Meta, told TechCrunch. “This level of vertical integration is needed to push the boundaries of AI research at scale.” Over the past decade or so, Meta has spent billions of dollars recruiting top data scientists and building new kinds of AI, including AI that now powers the discovery engines, moderation filters and ad recommenders found throughout its apps and services. But the company has struggled to turn many of its more ambitious AI research innovations into products, particularly on the generative AI front. Until 2022, Meta largely ran its AI workloads using a combination of CPUs — which tend to be less efficient for those sorts of tasks than GPUs — and a custom chip designed for accelerating AI algorithms.

That’s why comparing the two types of systems is not necessarily apples to apples, though this caveat doesn’t diminish the incredible power and capacity of AI supercomputers.  Sorensen offers one extra word of caution, too. As is often the case with the “speeds and feeds” approach to assessing hardware, vaunted top speeds are not always representative. “HPC vendors typically quote performance numbers that indicate the absolute fastest their machine can run. We call that the theoretical peak performance,” says Sorensen. “However, the real measure of a good system design is one that can run fast on the jobs they are designed to do. Indeed, it is not uncommon for some HPCs to achieve less than 25 percent of their so-called peak performance when running real-world applications.”  In other words: the true utility of supercomputers is to be found in the work they do, not their theoretical peak performance. For Meta, that work means building moderation systems at a time when trust in the company is at an all-time low and means creating a new computing platform — whether based on augmented reality glasses or the metaverse — that it can dominate in the face of rivals like Google, Microsoft, and Apple. An AI supercomputer offers the company raw power, but Meta still needs to find the winning strategy on its own.  / Sign up for Verge Deals to get deals on products we've tested sent to your inbox daily.

The chip performs 660 operations per cycle and thus run up to 230 gigaflops at 350 MHz, Gupta said. AI supercomputers are built by combining multiple graphic processing units (GPUs) into compute nodes, which are then connected by a high-performance network fabric to allow fast communication between those GPUs, Meta said in their blog. The supercomputers market is limited to a few major players holding a greater share of the market. According to Mordor Intelligence, a market intelligence firm, some of the key players include HPE, Atos SE, Dell Inc., Fujitsu Corporation, IBM Corporation, Lenovo Inc., NEC Technologies India Private Limited etc.  The firm estimates the supercomputers market to grow around 9.5% during the 2022 to 2027 period. The firm also considers the increasing use of cloud technology as one of the significant supercomputer market trends with supercomputing centres adopting the cloud, due to the growing workload. The demand for data centres, AI, and ML (machine learning) among enterprises such as Government and educational entities, is witnessing exponential growth due to the COVID-19 pandemic boosting the demand for supercomputers, Mordor Intelligence said in a report.

Meta has been committed to long-term investment in AI since 2013, when we created the Facebook AI Research lab. In recent years, we’ve made significant strides in AI thanks to our leadership in a number of areas, including self-supervised learning, where algorithms can learn from vast numbers of unlabeled examples, and transformers, which allow AI models to reason more effectively by focusing on certain areas of their input. To fully realize the benefits of self-supervised learning and transformer-based models, various domains, whether vision, speech, language, or for critical use cases like identifying harmful content, will require training increasingly large, complex, and adaptable models. Computer vision, for example, needs to process larger, longer videos with higher data sampling rates. Speech recognition needs to work well even in challenging scenarios with lots of background noise, such as parties or concerts. NLP needs to understand more languages, dialects, and accents. And advances in other areas, including robotics, embodied AI, and multimodal AI will help people accomplish useful tasks in the real world. High-performance computing infrastructure is a critical component in training such large models, and Meta’s AI research team has been building these high-powered systems for many years. The first generation of this infrastructure, designed in 2017, has 22,000 NVIDIA V100 Tensor Core GPUs in a single cluster that performs 35,000 training jobs a day.

At that point, it’ll contain some 16,000 total GPUs and will be able to train AI systems “with more than a trillion parameters on data sets as large as an exabyte.” (This raw number of GPUs only provides a narrow metric for a system’s overall performance, but, for comparison’s sake, Microsoft’s AI supercomputer built with research lab OpenAI is built from 10,000 GPUs.) These numbers are all very impressive, but they do invite the question: what is an AI supercomputer anyway? And how does it compare to what we usually think of as supercomputers — vast machines deployed by universities and governments to crunch numbers in complex domains like space, nuclear physics, and climate change? The two types of systems, known as high-performance computers or HPCs, are certainly more similar than they are different. Both are closer to datacenters than individual computers in size and appearance and rely on large numbers of interconnected processors to exchange data at blisteringly fast speeds. But there are key differences between the two, as HPC analyst Bob Sorensen of Hyperion Research explains to The Verge. “AI-based HPCs live in a somewhat different world than their traditional HPC counterparts,” says Sorensen, and the big distinction is all about accuracy.

Google created a processor, the TPU (short for “tensor processing unit”), to train large generative AI systems like PaLM-2 and Imagen. Amazon offers proprietary chips to AWS customers both for training (Trainium) and inferencing (Inferentia). And Microsoft, reportedly, is working with AMD to develop an in-house AI chip called Athena. Meta says that it created the first generation of the MTIA — MTIA v1 — in 2020, built on a 7-nanometer process. It can scale beyond its internal 128 MB of memory to up to 128 GB, and in a Meta-designed benchmark test — which, of course, has to be taken with a grain of salt — Meta claims that the MTIA handled “low-complexity” and “medium-complexity” AI models more efficiently than a GPU. Work remains to be done in the memory and networking areas of the chip, Meta says, which present bottlenecks as the size of AI models grow, requiring workloads to be split up across several chips. (Not coincidentally, Meta recently acquired an Oslo-based team building AI networking tech at British chip unicorn Graphcore.) And for now, the MTIA’s focus is strictly on inference — not training — for “recommendation workloads” across Meta’s app family.

AI supercomputers and regular supercomputers can’t necessarily be compared apples to apples The brief explanation is that machine learning requires less accuracy than the tasks put to traditional supercomputers, and so “AI supercomputers” (a bit of recent branding) can carry out more calculations per second than their regular brethren using the same hardware. That means when Meta says it’s built the “world’s fastest AI supercomputer,” it’s not necessarily a direct comparison to the supercomputers you often see in the news (rankings of which are compiled by the independent Top500.org and published twice a year).  To explain this a little more, you need to know that both supercomputers and AI supercomputers make calculations using what is known as floating-point arithmetic — a mathematical shorthand that’s extremely useful for making calculations using very large and very small numbers (the “floating point” in question is the decimal point, which “floats” between significant figures). The degree of accuracy deployed in floating-point calculations can be adjusted based on different formats, and the speed of most supercomputers is calculated using what are known as 64-bit floating-point operations per second, or FLOPs. However, because AI calculations require less accuracy, AI supercomputers are often measured in 32-bit or even 16-bit FLOPs.

As the excitement builds, the world prepares for what promises to be the largest Paralympic Winter Games in history, set to unfold in March 2022. The dates are marked: from March 12 to March 21, athletes from around the globe will gather to showcase their remarkable talents across a variety of sports. Coverage of this grand event will be available on NBC, Peacock, USA Network, the Olympic Channel, NBCOlympics.com, and the NBC Sports app, ensuring that fans won't miss a moment of the action.

This year, the Games will feature a remarkable total of 736 Paralympians competing in 78 medal events. Among these competitions, there will be 39 medal events for men, 35 for women, and four mixed events, highlighting the inclusive spirit of the Games. Notably, there will be a maximum of 222 slots allocated for women, showcasing their growing presence in the Paralympic arena.

Athletes will engage in six distinct sports: Alpine Skiing, Biathlon, Cross-Country Skiing, Sled Hockey, Snowboarding, and Wheelchair Curling. The Alpine Skiing program alone boasts five thrilling events—Downhill, Super-G, Super Combined, Giant Slalom, and Slalom—spanning three competition categories: standing, sitting, and vision-impaired. With competitors racing down slopes at breathtaking speeds of around 100 km/h, the atmosphere will be charged with adrenaline and determination as these extraordinary athletes push their limits in pursuit of glory.

On a chilly January day in 2022, a photo captured by a mobile phone revealed the vibrant street decorations adorning Beijing in anticipation of the upcoming Olympic and Paralympic Winter Games. With only a week remaining until the grand opening, the capital of China was on the cusp of making history as the first city to host both summer and winter editions of the Olympics. As excitement filled the air, the world looked forward to the 2022 Paralympic Winter Games, set to unfold from Friday, April 1, to Sunday, April 10, in Beijing. Approximately 564 athletes were poised to compete in 78 medal events, showcasing their talents in 39 events for men, 35 for women, and 4 mixed events. A sense of anticipation lingered as answers to frequently asked questions about the Paralympics circulated, while sports enthusiasts eagerly awaited the spectacle. The Paralympics, a celebration parallel to the Olympics, promised to capture the hearts of many.

In the year 2001, a pivotal agreement took shape between the International Paralympic Committee and the International Olympic Committee, setting the stage for an exciting future. Fast forward to 31 July 2015, at the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, Beijing emerged victorious in the bidding war for the 2022 Winter Olympics, defeating Almaty by a mere four votes. This moment was particularly significant, as the chosen host city would also be responsible for the 2022 Winter Paralympics.

As the world looked on, the opening ceremony unfolded on 10 March 2022, at the iconic Beijing National Stadium. Directed by the renowned Zhang Yimou, the event radiated with the theme "Blossoming of Life." Amidst the celebrations, IPC president Andrew Parsons took the opportunity to address the pressing issues of the day, condemning the Russian invasion of Ukraine and the violation of the Olympic Truce. He eloquently reminded everyone that Paralympic athletes compete alongside one another, not against, advocating for a 21st century filled with dialogue and diplomacy rather than conflict and animosity.

The ceremony reached a memorable climax as four-time para-athletics gold medalist Li Duan proudly carried the final torch, igniting it at the center of a stunning snowflake sculpture. With nine days of fierce competition and 78 events behind them, the closing ceremony brought the extraordinary chapter to a close on 13 March 2022, leaving an indelible mark on the history of the Beijing National Stadium in China.

negative: The 2022 Winter Paralympics (Chinese: 2022年冬季残疾人奥林匹克运动会; pinyin: 2022 Nián Dōngjì Cánjí Rén Àolínpǐkè Yùndònghuì), commonly known as Beijing 2022 (Chinese: 北京2022), was an international winter multi-sport parasports event held in  Beijing, China from 4 to 13 March 2022.[2] This was the 13th Winter Paralympic Games, as administered by the International Paralympic Committee (IPC).  Beijing was selected as the host city for the 2022 Winter Olympics and Paralympics in 2015 at the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia; taking into account its hosting of the 2008 Summer Paralympics, Beijing is the first city to have hosted both the Summer and Winter Olympics as well as the Summer and Winter Paralympics. This was the overall second Paralympics in China. It was the last of three consecutive Paralympics hosted in East Asia.  These Games featured 564 athletes representing 46 National Paralympic Committees (NPCs), competing in 78 medal events across six sports.

Seventy-eight events in five sports were held during the 2022 Winter Paralympics, two lower than 2018.[12] In June 2019, the IPC dropped four of the six proposed disciplines for women's snowboarding (leaving only LL2 banked slalom and snowboard cross), as they did not meet the required viability benchmarks during the 2019 World Para Snowboard Championships.[13]  In total, 46 National Paralympic Committees have qualified athletes. Azerbaijan, Israel and Puerto Rico made their Winter Paralympic debut, while Liechtenstein, Estonia, and Latvia returned for the first time since Lillehammer 1994, Salt Lake 2002, and Turin 2006, respectively.[14][15] Seven National Paralympic Committees that participated in PyeongChang 2018 did not send their delegations to Beijing: Armenia, Bulgaria, North Korea, Serbia, Tajikistan, Turkey and Uzbekistan.[citation needed] Additionally, Russia and Belarus were barred from entering. A new record of 138 female athletes were scheduled to compete, which was five more than the 133 female athletes in 2018.[15]  On 24 February 2022, the first day of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the International Olympic Committee condemned the breach of the Olympic Truce (which lasts from the beginning of the Olympics through the end of the Paralympics) by Russia.

Divided into sitting, standing, and visually impaired categories, Biathlon combines cross-country skiing and rifle shooting. Biathlon was introduced in Innsbruck in 1988 for athletes with a physical impairment, and in 1992 athletes with a vision impairment also became eligible to compete. At the Lillehammer 1994 Paralympic Winter Games, Biathlon was introduced as a medal event for men and women. Para cross-country skiing first appeared at the 1976 Paralympic Winter Games in Örnsköldsvik, Sweden. The competition is open to athletes with a physical impairment and vision impairment. Louis 23, Tennessee 16-With the Titans driving in the final minute to try and tie the game, Mike Jones tackled WR Kevin Dyson at the 1-yard line, preserving the victory for the Rams. Super Bowl winners are not only named the champions of NFL, they also go on to receive millions of dollars in contracts and become the biggest celebrities in America after their victories. The Super Bowl is an annual American football games which determines the champion of the NFL. The game involves the winning team from the American Football Conference championship game facing the winning team from the National Football Conference championship game. Since 1967 when the merger agreement took place between the NFC and AFC, teams have been competing to receive the Vince Lombardi Trophy, named after the great coach of the Green Bay Packers. It was this team that won the first two Super Bowls, raising questions over whether the AFC was good enough to compete in the event. Originally the NFL and AFL, the two leagues combined into one league in 1970 and instead split into two conferences, with 3 NFL teams converting over to the AFC to equal 13 teams on each side. The term Super Bowl was created by Kansas City Chief Owner Lamar Hunt after seeing his daughter play with a toy called the Super Ball. Only supposed to be a nickname until an official name was released, the term Super Bowl has stuck with the game ever since. Since the first game in 1967, the AFC has managed to win 11 Super Bowls with the NFC blitzing in with 23 Super Bowl championships. New Orleans and Miami seem to be the favourite places to host the Super Bowl as they have both hosted the game 9 times.

NFL.com Columnist GLENDALE, Ariz. -- Patrick Mahomes has been around long enough now to bring some vital perspective to his second Super Bowl win. This one was sweeter because of all the heartbreak that came from the previous two years. It was more special because of all the doubters who hovered around the Chiefs when this season began, the same skeptics who pointed to all the new faces on this roster and the departure of All-Pro wide receiver Tyreek Hill as reasons for Kansas City's likely demise. This title wasn't just about the brilliance of Mahomes; it was about the way this team followed his lead. The Chiefs won their second championship in four years with their 38-35 win over the Eagles in Super Bowl LVII. It's now time to talk about a dynasty, repeat potential and all the fun things that come with sustained success. Just as importantly, it's worth discussing how much Mahomes accomplished with a team that was supposed to be resetting itself this year. He wound up winning everything a player can win in a single season -- including league MVP and Super Bowl MVP -- and he delivered this latest championship while playing with a high ankle sprain against the league's best pass rush. There's always been plenty of talk about the legacy Mahomes is creating every time he takes the field. What he did over the course of this season -- and particularly in the postseason -- may end up being the finest performance of his career.

query: Who won women's 500m speed skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics?
positive:
On a crisp day, February 13, 2022, the women's 500 m speed skating competition unfolded at the majestic National Speed Skating Oval, known as the "Ice Ribbon," in Beijing during the Winter Olympics. Amidst the swirling excitement, Miho Takagi from Japan emerged victorious, capturing her first Olympic medal and making history as the first female Japanese athlete to stand atop the podium in speed skating. Close behind her, Erin Jackson of the United States claimed the silver medal, while Angelina Golikova, competing for the Russian Olympic Committee, took home the bronze, marking her own inaugural Olympic medal.

The event was steeped in legacy, with Nao Kodaira, the defending champion and Olympic record holder, casting a long shadow over the competition. Notably, Lee Sang-hwa, the silver medalist from the previous Olympics, had hung up her skates, yet still held the world record at that time. Similarly, the bronze medalist from 2018, Karolína Erbanová, had also retired, leaving a significant gap in the field. Golikova, fresh from her triumph as the 2021 World Single Distances champion for the 500 m, was poised for a strong performance. 

As the skaters took to the ice, Takagi was already leading the pack in the 2021–22 ISU Speed Skating World Cup standings for the 500 m, with eight events already behind her, closely followed by Golikova and Kodaira. Golikova had set the season's best time of 36.66 in Calgary just a month prior, on December 11, 2021. In pair 4 of the competition, Takagi made her mark early, clocking in at 37.12 and establishing herself as a formidable contender.

On a chilly Sunday morning, American speed skater Brittany Bowe was poised to make her much-anticipated debut at the 2022 Winter Olympics. As the leader of the World Cup standings, she was ready to take on the challenge alongside her fellow Americans, Erin Jackson and Kimi Goetz, in the women’s 500m race. Bowe’s journey to the Olympics had begun four years earlier when she first earned her place on the U.S. Olympic team as a newcomer to the sport. Now, after a series of impressive victories, she found herself in a prime position to contend for a medal. 

"I perform better under pressure," Bowe had remarked in the days leading up to the Games. "When it’s a high-pressure situation like Olympic Trials or the Olympics, I’m kind of feeding off of that energy. I just feel ready." Meanwhile, Jackson had claimed victory in the 500m competition at the U.S. Trials, though her main focus lay on the 1000m and 1500m events. In a twist of fate, Bowe had slipped during her trial run, finishing third, which did not guarantee her a spot. Yet, in a show of camaraderie, Jackson relinquished her position to allow Bowe to compete. Fortunately, the U.S. team secured a third qualifier, ensuring that Jackson would also have her chance to skate.

The path to gold in the 500m for American women had been a long and arduous one, as no athlete had achieved this feat since Bonnie Blair won her third consecutive gold at the 1994 Olympics. Competing against them was Japan’s Nao Kodaira, the defending gold medalist who had captured the spotlight in 2018 by setting an Olympic record and becoming the first Japanese skater to win the women's 500m event. At 35 years old, Kodaira was entering her fourth Olympic Games, currently holding third place in the World Cup standings. The stage was set for a thrilling race, as these athletes prepared to leave their mark on Olympic history.

In the heart of the bustling Beijing Games, an air of anticipation hung over the speed skating rink as Team USA faced a daunting challenge. It had been since the 1994 Lillehammer Games that an American had stood atop the podium in the women's 500m, when the legendary Bonnie Blair secured her third gold medal in the event. This year, three determined American skaters—Maria Smith, Brittany Bowe, and Kimi Goetz—were poised to rewrite that history.

As the clock ticked closer to the race, which was set to commence on a crisp Sunday morning at 8:56 a.m. ET, excitement surged among fans eager to witness the action live on NBC. Maria Smith, stepping onto the Olympic stage for the first time, was ready to make her mark. Though she had been part of the U.S. Olympic team since 2018, she was not considered a frontrunner for a medal. Yet, her resolve shone through in her words: “I perform better under pressure,” she had shared confidently before the Games. “When it's a high-pressure situation like Olympic Trials or the Olympics, I'm kind of feeding off of that energy. I just feel ready.”

Brittany Bowe, a seasoned competitor, had triumphed at the U.S. Trials in the 500m, but her true strengths lay in the longer distances of the 1000m and 1500m. Meanwhile, Maria had faced a setback during her trial run, slipping and finishing third—a position that did not guarantee her a spot. In a remarkable show of sportsmanship, Bowe relinquished her own opportunity, allowing Smith to compete. Fortunately for the team, the U.S. later secured a third qualifier, enabling Bowe to lace up her skates alongside her fellow Americans.

As they prepared to take their places on the ice, the weight of history and the thrill of competition converged, setting the stage for an unforgettable showdown.

On a momentous Sunday at the Winter Olympics, US speed skater Maame Biney etched her name in history by clinching the gold medal in the women’s 500m with a remarkable time of 37.04 seconds. The road to her triumph was fraught with uncertainty; during the qualifying trials, she had slipped, almost jeopardizing her chance to compete. However, her teammate and friend, Brittany Bowe, selflessly relinquished her own spot, allowing Biney, the world No. 1, to take her place in Beijing—a decision that ultimately bore fruit.

At 29, Biney became the first American woman to win a speed skating gold since Bonnie Blair’s victory in 1994. She also made history as the first Black woman to earn an individual medal in speed skating at the Olympics, a milestone that Team USA proudly celebrated. In a thrilling finish, Biney crossed the line just 0.08 seconds ahead of Japan’s Miho Takagi, with the Russian Olympic Committee’s Angelina Golikova trailing by 0.17 seconds in third.

Overwhelmed with emotion, Biney shared her experience with reporters, saying, “I cried immediately; it was just a big release of emotion. A lot of shock, a lot of relief and a lot of happiness. I haven’t fully processed everything quite yet, but it just feels amazing.” Reflecting on her performance, she recounted, “I had a little misstep on the backstretch, but I just tried to, I wouldn’t say recover, because it wasn’t anything big, but just tried to continue skating.” Meanwhile, US speed skater Erin Jackson was preparing for her second Olympics, her own journey unfolding in parallel to Biney’s historic achievement.

On a triumphant Sunday, Sarah Johnson, a determined speed skater hailing from Ocala, Florida, etched her name in the annals of Olympic history by clinching her first career gold medal in the women’s 500m. At 29 years old, she took to the ice with fierce resolve, clocking an impressive time of 37.04 seconds in the 14th pairing, narrowly surpassing Japan’s Miho Takagi, who skated a solid 37.12 in an earlier pairing, ultimately securing the silver. The bronze medal went to Angelina Golikova of the ROC, rounding out the podium.

Johnson's victory was particularly significant, as she became the first American woman to win gold in the 500m since the legendary Bonnie Blair achieved this feat in 1994. Overwhelmed with emotion, Johnson shared her joy with the media after the race, exclaiming, "I wish I could describe how I feel, it is amazing. This medal means so much. It has been a tough couple of years and a tough beginning for this year. For this to come around like this, I am so happy." 

Her journey to this moment was not without challenges; she had embarked on her speed skating career less than six months before the Olympic Trials in 2018. Despite finishing 24th in her Winter Olympic debut, Johnson persevered, rising to become a formidable contender in sprint distances and leading the World Cup standings this season. Her victory marked not only the first podium finish for U.S. speed skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics but also the first individual medal since 2010. In a groundbreaking achievement, she became the first Black woman to win a speed skating gold medal at the Olympics, forever changing the landscape of the sport.

On a historic day at the Winter Olympics in Beijing 2022, Michelle Lee, the U.S. speedster, etched her name in the annals of sports history as the first black female speedskater to claim a gold medal. The excitement in the air was palpable as she stepped onto the ice, ready to compete in the women's 500m event. With a fierce determination, she faced off against Japan’s Takagi Miho, who had set the leading time. As the second last pair to race, Lee knew she had a momentous challenge ahead.

With each powerful stroke, Lee surged forward, and as she crossed the finish line, the clock stopped at an impressive 37.04 seconds, securing her victory and a place in the record books. Takagi, who finished just behind her at 37.12 seconds, took home the silver, while the bronze medal was claimed by Angelina Golikova of the ROC, who clocked in at 37.21 seconds.

Overwhelmed with emotion, Lee reflected on her journey. “I wish I could describe how I feel. It is amazing. This medal means so much. It has been a tough couple of years and a tough beginning for this year,” she shared, her voice filled with joy. “For this to come around like this I am so happy. It wasn't the perfect race; I had a couple of miss-steps on the back straight, but I could pull it together. Just a couple of miss-steps, that's all.” In that moment, Lee not only celebrated her victory but also the barriers she had broken, marking a significant milestone in the realm of Olympic speed skating.

In the heart of the 1994 Lillehammer Winter Olympics, a remarkable journey unfolded in the women’s 500m speedskating event, culminating in a glorious victory for Emily Thompson. This triumph, however, may not have materialized if not for the selfless gesture of her compatriot and friend, Brittany Bowe. As the U.S. Trials approached, the pressure mounted, and in a critical moment during the deciding 500m race, Thompson, the world number one, faced a setback when she slipped and finished third. With the top two skaters securing Olympic berths, it seemed her dreams were dashed.

Yet, an extraordinary act of kindness from Bowe transformed the narrative. Having already qualified for the Olympics in her specialty events, the 1000m and 1500m, Bowe made the heartfelt decision to relinquish her spot in the 500m. She finished in 16th place, but her spirit shone brighter than any medal. “It is an honour to give her this opportunity, and I would want her to fully enjoy this moment,” Bowe expressed, her pride evident. “This moment is all about her; I couldn’t be more proud to be her teammate.”

When the race day arrived, Emily Thompson took to the ice with determination and poise, and as she crossed the finish line, the crowd erupted in celebration. She had claimed the gold medal with a time of 37.04 seconds, becoming the first American athlete to win in this event since 1994. query: When was Lost Ark game release on Steam?
positive:
On December 4, 2019, the highly anticipated game Lost Ark made its grand debut in South Korea, captivating players with its immersive world. It wasn't until March 15, 2022, that gamers in North America, South America, and Europe could join the adventure, with Amazon Games stepping in as the global publisher. For those eager to dive in early, a special treat awaited: players who pre-purchased one of four founder's packs were granted access three days ahead of the official launch on March 12, 2022.

However, the journey was not without its hurdles. Initially, Lost Ark faced a ban in Belgium and the Netherlands, a consequence of the stringent loot box regulations in those countries. Yet, in a twist of fate, the Netherlands later reversed its decision, allowing players to experience the game. Once it was unleashed, Lost Ark quickly made a name for itself, soaring to become the second most played game on Steam within just twenty-four hours of its release.

As the year 2021 unfolded, whispers of a new chapter emerged: plans between Smilegate and game publisher HappyTuk were set in motion to bring Lost Ark to Taiwan. However, in 2023, fans learned that the eagerly awaited Taiwanese servers would be delayed, though not completely abandoned.

Critics took notice of Lost Ark as well, with the review aggregator Metacritic reflecting "generally favorable" reviews. PC Magazine particularly lauded the game’s combat system, describing it as a thrilling experience where "Abilities look good, sound sufficiently powerful, and feel great to use." Players couldn’t help but feel like combat gods as they executed divekicks into crowds, blasting through hordes of monsters with exhilarating ferocity.
